reasoning_model_1: The model correctly interprets the instruction to include non-linguistic vocalizations. Instead of reading the words 'Ahem, cough, cough', it generates realistic audio events corresponding to these descriptions between 0:00 and 0:03. The sound effects of throat clearing and coughing are very natural and high-quality. However, it is important to note a significant content accuracy issue: the spoken part of the script 'I'd like to say a few words' was translated and spoken in Chinese ('我想说几句') instead of English. Despite this language error in the verbal content, the specific paralinguistic feature (Non-Linguistic Vocalizations) requested was executed perfectly.
reasoning_model_2: The model failed to generate the required non-linguistic vocalizations. Instead of producing the sounds of coughing or throat clearing, it literally read the onomatopoeic words 'Ahem' and 'Cough' as text between 0:00 and 0:02. Furthermore, it missed the second 'cough' from the script and misread 'I'd like' as 'I like'. Since the model merely read the text without converting the designated segments into sound effects, it failed the core requirement of the evaluation dimension.
model_comparison: Model 1 (T1) is the clear winner regarding the specific evaluation dimension 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations'. T1 successfully generated realistic and natural coughing and throat-clearing sounds as requested by the prompt, whereas Model 2 (T2) simply read the words 'Ahem' and 'Cough' literally, completely failing to produce the paralinguistic feature. Although T1 hallucinated the language of the spoken text into Chinese (a content error), its performance on the target feature (generating the sound of coughing) was vivid and accurate. T2 failed to exhibit the feature entirely.
